overview: Turn of River takes its name from the bend in the Rippowam River that marks its eastern boundary — a geographic feature documented since the colonial era and commemorated by a lenticular truss bridge listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1987. The neighborhood spreads across north-central Stamford with a suburban character defined by half-acre to acre-and-a-half lots, mature tree canopy, and a volunteer fire department that has served the community since 1928. Turn of River Middle School and proximity to the High Ridge Road and Bull's Head commercial corridors provide daily infrastructure without requiring a trip downtown.

children.text: The Rippowam River flows seventeen miles from Ridgefield to Stamford Harbor, draining a catchment area of nearly forty square miles. Where it bends sharply through the northern reaches of Stamford, it gives Turn of River both its name and its eastern boundary. The Turn-of-River Bridge — a single-span lenticular pony truss built by the Berlin Iron Bridge Company in 1892 — still crosses the river on Old North Stamford Road, a surviving piece of nineteenth-century engineering in a neighborhood otherwise defined by mid-twentieth-century residential development. That juxtaposition of old infrastructure and postwar housing gives the area a layered character.

The Turn of River Fire Department, a volunteer organization founded in 1928, operates the largest volunteer apparatus fleet in Stamford — a heavy rescue unit, two engines, two tankers, and a ladder truck. The department's continued volunteer status is unusual for a city of Stamford's size and reflects the neighborhood's self-reliant identity. Turn of River Middle School serves the area's student population and anchors the residential community alongside the fire station. Together, these institutions establish a civic presence that functions independently of downtown Stamford's municipal core.

Westover Road and Route 104 form the neighborhood's primary corridors, connecting residents to two commercial nodes. High Ridge Road runs through the district's western edge with Trader Joe's, Stop & Shop, coffeehouses, and specialty shops lining the thoroughfare. Bull's Head, a few minutes south, adds the Bull's Head Diner — a Stamford institution serving everything from Greek specialties to grilled seafood — alongside hardware stores, pharmacies, and service businesses. The dual proximity means Turn of River residents can handle most errands without crossing into downtown Stamford or merging onto a highway.

Lot sizes are the defining advantage. Where central Stamford neighborhoods compress homes onto eighth-acre and quarter-acre parcels, Turn of River properties typically sit on half an acre to an acre and a half. Colonials, raised ranches, and split-levels from the 1950s through 1970s are the dominant styles, many retaining their original footprints while others have been expanded with additions and updated mechanical systems. The price band of $600,000 to $1.1 million delivers significantly more land per dollar than any Stamford neighborhood south of the Merritt Parkway. For buyers prioritizing outdoor space, privacy, and suburban scale, this is where the math works.

highlights: The Turn-of-River Bridge, built in 1892 by the Berlin Iron Bridge Company, is a National Register-listed lenticular truss spanning the Rippowam River on Old North Stamford Road
Half-acre to acre-and-a-half lot sizes deliver more land per dollar than any Stamford neighborhood south of the Merritt Parkway
High Ridge Road and Bull's Head commercial nodes provide Trader Joe's, Stop & Shop, the Bull's Head Diner, and specialty retail within minutes
Turn of River Fire Department, a volunteer company since 1928, operates the largest volunteer apparatus fleet in Stamford with six units
housingCharacteristics:
Turn of River's housing stock dates primarily from the 1950s through the 1970s, when the neighborhood was subdivided into residential lots that are generous by Stamford standards. Colonials are the most prevalent style — typically four-bedroom homes with attached two-car garages on half-acre to one-acre lots. Raised ranches and split-levels account for a significant share of inventory, offering open main-level layouts with finished lower levels that add usable square footage.

Many homes retain their original exteriors and floor plans, presenting renovation opportunity for buyers willing to update kitchens, bathrooms, and windows. A growing number of properties have been substantially expanded or rebuilt, particularly along the streets closest to High Ridge Road. New construction is limited because the neighborhood is largely built out, but the oversized lots occasionally attract developers who propose replacement homes. Prices range from $600,000 for original-condition raised ranches to approximately $1.1 million for fully renovated colonials on the largest parcels.
marketNotes:
Turn of River attracts buyers who prioritize space over proximity to the waterfront or downtown. The typical purchaser is a family moving up from a Stamford condo or a smaller home in a denser neighborhood, looking for a yard, a garage, and room for children to grow. Competition is sharpest in the $700,000 to $900,000 range, where updated colonials on three-quarter-acre lots represent the neighborhood's sweet spot.

Original-condition homes below $700,000 draw renovation-minded buyers and occasional investors. Above $1 million, the buyer pool thins and overlaps with North Stamford, where even larger lots are available at comparable prices. Sellers who invest in kitchen and bathroom updates before listing tend to recoup the expense through faster sales and stronger offers. Inventory turns over steadily but without the urgency seen in waterfront or train-adjacent neighborhoods, giving buyers slightly more negotiating room.
locationAccess:
Turn of River sits in north-central Stamford with the Merritt Parkway accessible via Route 104 and High Ridge Road. The parkway provides a scenic, truck-free route to Fairfield County towns to the east and Westchester County to the west. Interstate 95 is reachable in approximately ten minutes via High Ridge Road south through Bull's Head.

The Stamford Transportation Center, serving Metro-North Railroad and Amtrak, is roughly a fifteen-minute drive south. High Ridge Road's commercial strip runs directly through the neighborhood's western edge, and the Bull's Head intersection sits just south, concentrating grocery stores, dining, and professional services along a single corridor. Downtown Stamford is ten to fifteen minutes by car. Greenwich Avenue is approximately twenty minutes west via the Merritt Parkway.

faqs.question: What is the price range for homes in Turn of River, Stamford CT?
faqs.answer: Turn of River homes generally trade between $600,000 and $1.1 million. Original-condition raised ranches and split-levels on half-acre lots occupy the lower end of that range, while fully renovated colonials on parcels approaching an acre and a half reach the upper tier. The price-per-acre ratio in Turn of River is among the most favorable in Stamford, making it a strong option for families and professionals prioritizing lot size and outdoor space.

faqs.question: What shopping and dining options are near Turn of River?
faqs.answer: Two commercial corridors serve Turn of River. High Ridge Road runs along the neighborhood's western edge with Trader Joe's, Stop & Shop, coffeehouses, boutiques, and specialty retailers. Bull's Head, a few minutes south, adds the Bull's Head Diner — a longstanding Stamford dining institution — alongside hardware stores, pharmacies, and service businesses. Both corridors are reachable without highway driving.

faqs.question: How large are the lots in Turn of River compared to other Stamford neighborhoods?
faqs.answer: Turn of River lots typically range from half an acre to an acre and a half, which is substantially larger than what is available in central, southern, or waterfront Stamford neighborhoods where quarter-acre and eighth-acre parcels are common. The oversized lots support detached garages, mature landscaping, and backyard space that would be rare in neighborhoods closer to downtown or the coastline.
